item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What happens to people who are exposed to the toxin but not the anemone? I feel like there’s an opportunity for more weird here in the form of a test log. Maybe even something the foundation could weaponize, especially since it works on various organisms.

item.title: WMTA
item.content:encoded:
What makes this anomalous?

I like that you go into so much detail. But at the same time, you've gone into too much scientific detail. You've explained all the anomaly away. Sure the actinotoxin might be anomalous, but if you start classifying all scientifically unexplained chemicals as anomalous, then the mainlist would stretch into infinity.

Personally I don't think it's anomalous, because an effect like the one described could feasibly be induced by a chemical, just one that we haven't discovered yet. It fits inside the defined model for what chemicals can possibly do. Hand it over to the marine biologists and declassify it, I say! But no hard feelings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While this article is technically competent in terms of adhering to tone and basic structure, I feel it is extremely lacking in terms of originality. Where others would use these tropes (compulsive interaction, body horror) in service of certain narratives or as traits of a more interesting subject, here it is the main course; there isn't much for me to get invested in beyond the basic squickiness of [DATA EXPUNGING] an anemone.

This does not seem like the type of article that should have been a viable mainlist submission given our general aptitude to produce fresh and original content.

I would like to hear thoughts from others on why they believe this does or does not pass muster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I generally liked this article I mean the weirder the better so when I saw the title I was intrigued this is one of the articles that are gross but not too an extent where it diverts the readers attention good job oh yeah and PS I intend on using the 3216 spot so that could make us neighbours

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this! I don't think it needs anything more than what it is right now, honestly; it's cute little articles like this that got me into SCP stuff and it's nice to read new stuff like them.

Only suggestion I might have is either deleting the [Data Expunged] or un-expunging it. The foundation has lots of sex monsters out there, and it doesn't make sense for this database of extreme horrors to get super up-tight about sex of all things.

But I really do love this, it's short and sweet and paints a nice picture of this weird anomalous predatory organism that got scooped out of whatever plane it lived in and put in a pet shop.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your measurements don't make sense; you're measuring volume in meters and meters squared, rather than meters cubed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was the addendums that made me really like this. The idea of a potentially deadly monstrosity being sold in a pet shop with care instructions to follow to avoid horrible death just appeals to me for some reason. Reminds me of the Mogwai from Gremlins, or the creatures from Pet Shop of Horrors. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your comment slayed me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No bias or anything :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good changes. However, as I reread the article, I'm struck by how closely it resembles SCP-2790, from being a sea creature to its effects on people to its place of origin - and on the whole, SCP-2790 has a much darker and more humorous ramp-up. This ends up being a much less interesting variant, which forces me to downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Affected organisms will attempt to touch, grab, consume and [DATA EXPUNGED] sections of SCP-3215

Don't fuck the sea anemone. D:

Other than that, this got no reaction out of me. It's just an OP animal. :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I struggled when brainstorming for extra content, such as experiment logs, because I was hoping to keep its effects straightforward and instead put the focus on its story - the mysterious nature of its appearance in a little marine store in Florida.

Do you have any suggestions for expansions that could be made?

EDIT: Actually, I have a scrapped idea about an experiment log that would suggest how SCP-3215 came to find its way into a tank (via a form of contamination). Would that be a reasonable addition?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is competent, but its effects aren't interesting enough to carry it. I would consider adding an experiment log or something to expand on it a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea came from a relative fear (or, at the least, cringe factor) involving deep-sea organisms that I appear to suffer from. The squidgy anemones, especially, make my skin crawl.

I was hoping to reference this fear in my article. As far as I could see, eating one of the things would be the absolute weirdest and worst thing that I could imagine doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the feedback!

Yeah, sorry about that. I know that redaction shouldn't really be in the Special Containment Procedures, but in the first draft it actually said "Site Director ████████" which I blanked out so as to not to interfere with possible Foundation lore by giving a random name to the director of a Site which is referenced in other SCP articles.

I changed "Site Director" to "Lead Researcher" in order to negate the need for this redaction, then completely forgot to add a name at all. Whoops.

Change made as needed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback! Made improvements to the bits you cited.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Perhaps the nature of the compulsion here is fitting, but it's kinda uninteresting as an anomaly. And that appears to be a significant portion of the article's content.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The compulsion effect works well here, so kudos. However, this is terrible redacting:

The code for said lock is to be changed semi-frequently and can be granted to research personnel by Lead Researcher ████████.

"Hey, John, I need to collect some samples of 3215. D'you know who to talk to about opening its tank?"

"Yeah, let me check. Uh, it's Lead Researcher… Blackbox."

"Is that Researcher Blackbox or Researcher Blackbox?"

"No, you're thinking of Researchers Blackbox and Blackbox. I'm talking about Researcher Blackbox."

Redaction in the Containment Procedures should be kept to a minimum - and when it's done, there had better be a damn good in-universe reason to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, an anemone that people obsess over and do nasty stuff to? Ew. +1

I also like that the anomaly in here is understated and not something with immediately world-shattering effects.

Grammar:

+ show block
– hide block

unidentifiable species

stimulates itchiness

I think "unidentified" and "causes itchiness" would be a better word choice here.

During this time, subjects identify SCP-3215 as a meal of some description, often to elucidate and declare that it will be, or is, the best food that they have ever eaten.

I'd phrase this as "During this time, subjects identify SCP-3215 as a meal of some description, often elucidating and declaring that it will be, or is, the best food that they have ever eaten."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It isn't? I'm really sorry about that - the warning at the top of Series IV has been removed and places besides the contest entries seem to have begun to be filled. I also saw in one of the discussions that Series IV was open for business. I think I misunderstood.

What are the actions I should take to relocate my article?

Oh, no worries! Thanks for taking the time to let me know for sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Series 4 isn't open yet. My mistake, apparently Series 4 IS open. Maybe next time I should check before opening my mouth.

item.title: Images
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the taking the time to read my article!

Both images are sourced from Pixabay and used under Creative Commons license.
